All of the thousands of WhatsApp messages were read out in court, showing how his infatuation and desperation grew.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“They showed that he was completely obsessed with her,” says Glenda. “It was only at trial that I realised that she was being stalked. There was this coercive control that she didn’t realise he was doing, because she was in control of her life… until she wasn’t.”

WhatsApp Obsession: The Murder of Stephanie Hansen, airs on Tuesday on ITV1.

News.title: Iran secretly agrees to give up nuke plans, Trump says after talks avert huge blitz – but regime DENIES negotiations
News.content:
IRAN has secretly agreed to give up its nuclear ambitions, Donald Trump has claimed – as the regime denies talks have taken place.

The US president earlier called off a major blitz of Iran’s energy network after holding “very good and productive conversations” with Tehran.





9 Credit: truthsocial

Trump had vowed to “decimate” the country’s power plants if they did not reopen the Strait of Hormuz by midnight.

Speaking to reporters outside Air Force One, Trump added that in order for talks to be successful, Iran would need to have “no more nuclear weapons”.

“That’s point number one, two and three,” he said

“They will never have a nuclear weapon.”

Asked whether Iran would relinquish its nuclear ambitions, Trump replied: “They’ve agreed to that.”

It comes as…



But Iran’s Fars news agency has flatly denied that direct talks had occurred between the warring nations.

It has also vowed to continue wreaking havoc on the global economy with its blockade of the strait.

Fuel prices have rocketed from under $73 (£55) a barrel on the eve of the conflict to $113 (£85), sparking fears of a worldwide recession.

Tehran accused Trump of backing down only after the murderous regime threatened to blitz neighbouring countries hosting US bases.

Trump later rebuked Iran’s claim, telling reporters: “They called, I didn’t call. They want to make a deal.”



And he suggested that it had been Iran who capitulated out of fear of US strikes.

“Tomorrow morning, sometime their time, we were expecting to blow up their largest electric generating plants that cost over 10 billion US dollars to build.

“It was a very good one, there was no dearth of money. One shot it is gone. It collapses. Why would they want that?

“So they called, I didn’t call. They called. They want to make a deal.



Trump said he had ordered the Department of War to pause any planned strikes on Iranian power plants and energy infrastructure for five days, while negotiations continue and depending on their success.

Downing Street praised Trump’s announcement, with a No10 spokesperson saying: “Any reports of productive talks are welcome.

“We have always said that a swift resolution to the war is in global interests and the Strait of Hormuz specifically needs to be reopened.”

They also shed some light on the phone call between Sir Keir Starmer and the US president on Sunday, explaining that the pair specifically discussed the Strait of Hormuz.



Despite Trump’s declaration that he would postpone attacks on energy facilities, Israel announced a fresh wave of strikes in Tehran on Monday.

Trump had issued the 48-hour ultimatum on Saturday and doubled down on the threat in a fiery interview, saying: “You’ll find out what’s gonna happen. You’re gonna find out soon.

“Total decimation of Iran. It’s gonna work out very good.”

Iran’s regime hit back with chilling threats of its own, warning that US bases and energy sites across the Middle East would become targets if America struck.



Power plants in Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, UAE, Saudi Arabia, Jordan, Iraq, Syria and Turkey were all named in the firing line.

Lieutenant Colonel Ebrahim Zolfaqari said: “Everything is ready for a major jihad aimed at the total destruction of US economic interests in West Asia.

“We did not start this war and will not initiate it now, but if the enemy harms our power plants, we will do whatever is necessary to defend our country and national interests.”

Tensions had already exploded after Iran fired missiles towards the US-UK base on Diego Garcia in a major escalation that stunned military chiefs.

One missile failed, while another was shot down by a US warship – but the attack raised fears Tehran can now strike targets up to 4,000km away.



The twisted regime had also issued a chilling warning that even tourist hotspots could be hit.

IRGC General Abolfazl Shekarchi said: “From now on… even parks, recreational areas, and tourist destinations anywhere in the world will no longer be safe for you.”

Meanwhile, the US had been gearing up for a major offensive.

Around 2,500 Marines were deployed to the region, while Navy SEALs, submarines and stealth helicopters were put on standby for a high-risk mission.

Plans were even being drawn up to seize Kharg Island – Iran’s key oil export hub – in a move designed to choke off the regime’s finances.



Military chiefs have insisted Iran was already faltering.

Admiral Brad Cooper, commander of US Central Command, said: “They’re operating in a sign of desperation.

“In the last couple of weeks, they’ve attacked civilian targets very deliberately, more than 300 times.”



RECOMMENDED STORIES

He added Iran’s firepower was dwindling: “At the beginning of the conflict, you saw large volumes in the dozens of drones and missiles.

“You no longer see that. It’s all one or two at a time.”

News.title: Teen farm worker crushed to death after getting trapped inside hay bale machine, inquest hears
News.content:
FARM TRAGEDY Teen farm worker crushed to death after getting trapped inside hay bale machine, inquest hears

A TEENAGE farm worker was crushed to death in a bale wrapping machine – as his older brother worked in an adjacent field, an inquest heard.

Luke Searle, 17, had been operating a tractor and baling machine to wrap up cut grass bales used for animal feed.



But an inquest heard that there was a problem with the bale wrapper and Luke went to investigate but became caught in the machine.



The inquest at Exeter Coroner’s Court heard that farm owner James Williams went to check on Luke and found the machinery was not moving in the field.

He discovered Luke trapped and unconscious in the baler.

Mr Williams, who operates a partnership including five farms and 2,000 acres, called for help which included Luke’s older brother William racing to the field.

They freed Luke from the machine, gave him CPR but he was pronounced dead at the scene.

The inquest heard that Luke’s family and the farm owners, the Williams, had been close friends for many years.

Luke was working at Sherwell Farm near Plympton, Devon, in June 2022 when the tragic incident happened at around 8pm one evening.

He was a student at Cornwall based Duchy College on a two year agricultural machinery land-based engineering course with 80% of the course being on the farm.

Health and Safety Executive inspector Simon Jones told the hearing there had been a problem with the bale wrapper arms not rotating and Luke went to investigate the fault.



But as he did so the arms started again in an “unexpected and unplanned movement” trapping him and crushing him.

He had not put the machinery into a safe mode before investigating the fault, the inquest was told.

The jury heard the power should be isolated, the engine switched off and controls put in neutral before any repair.

Nicholas Lane, the Area Coroner, told the inquest jury that the Searle brothers were very close and had a rural upbringing and planned careers in farming.

They heard Luke was “a very sociable well liked young man who loved what he was doing”.



The inquest continues and will last several days.

News.title: Dealer & YouTube gangster dubbed ‘The General’ who UK can’t deport because no country will take him is arrested AGAIN
News.content:
BUSTED AGAIN Dealer & YouTube gangster dubbed ‘The General’ who UK can’t deport because no country will take him is arrested AGAIN

A NOTORIOUS gang leader known as The General has been arrested again – after the UK failed to deport him as no country will take him.

Joland Giwa has boasted about his crimes as a member of the Croydon-based crime ring Don’t Say Nothing (DSN) on YouTube for years.





The 36-year-old arrived in the UK when he was just 10 and he is believed to be from either Nigeria or Sierra Leone.

But, the Home Office has been unable to deport the criminal because neither country will take him.

Officials instead decided that Giwa would be sent to Newport, in Wales, to cut him off from his criminal connections in London.



Giwa was sentenced to 27 months in February 2009 after committing a string of robberies and thefts.

He ended up living in a bail hostel in Newport after serving his time.

The YouTuber found himself locked up again in 2015 for a terrifying robbery at a William Hill bookies in Ringland.

The late former Labour MP Paul Flynn said at the time: “There was outrage when a man described by a court and the police as so “dangerous” they didn’t want him in London was sent to Newport.



“Since coming to this city he has committed [a] violent robbery.

“Newport has a long tradition of proud achievement in assimilating immigrants but if you start putting excessive numbers in a place you get excessive pressures.

“There ought to be a more even spread across the country.”

Giwa was nicked once again in 2020 for trafficking drugs into Newport and was jailed for four years.

Officers had caught the gangster selling drugs at a children’s play park after arriving on an electric scooter.



The court heard Giwa was caught with class A drugs with a potential street value of £4,700, £1,300 in cash and three mobile phones.

The defendant admitted possession with intent to supply heroin, possession with intent to supply crack cocaine and possession with intent to supply cocaine.

Giwa has now been busted again for the same offences, which took place on February 17 this year.

The defendant found himself back in Cardiff Crown Court and pleaded guilty.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Giwa will be sentenced on April 10 and he has been remanded in custody.



The Home Office has been asked to comment.

News.title: Iran vows to unleash 'new surprises' after threatening to bomb tourist hotspots
News.content:
Donald Trump should "take his head out of his mobile and look at the sky" as Iran is set to unveil "new surprises" in the war, a military source has said.

It comes just days after Iran's top military spokesman warned "parks, recreational areas and tourist destinations" worldwide won't be safe for the country's enemies.

"From now on, based on the information we have about you, even parks, recreational areas and tourist destinations anywhere in the world will no longer be safe for you," Abolfazl Shekarchi said, according to Iranian state television.

Today, further threats were made after Trump claimed he had postponed "any and all" military strikes against Iran following "very good and productive" talks to resolve the ongoing conflict across the Middle East. He added that the talks had headed towards a "complete and total resolution of our hostilities" in the region.

Image: (Image: Jake Paul/YouTube)) (Image: Jake Paul/YouTube))

An Iranian military source now says the US president "sees all his military options in the war as defeated and is looking for a way out of the stalemate.

"Trump knows that his offensive and defensive military ammunition is in a very poor state, and he has no hope of a military victory or of reopening the Strait of Hormuz; therefore, with his military ammunition running low, he has resorted to firing words, which is only bringing him further disgrace.

"Contrary to Trump's empty promises, Iran has prepared surprises for the coming days that will make the outcome of the war even clearer," the source told state media.

"Trump should take his head out of his phone and social media for a bit, and from now on, he should just keep his eye on the sky and the stock markets, and the price of oil," the military official added.

This is a breaking news story.

News.title: 'Golders Green ambulance explosions threw me backwards - I felt them in my guts'
News.content:
EXCLUSIVE: 'Golders Green ambulance explosions threw me backwards - I felt them in my guts'

Dad Max Reisner ran from his home in Golders Green, north London, when he realised ambulances were on fire - and took shocking footage as they exploded. Police are hunting the arsonists

EXCLUSIVE: Huge explosions heard as ambulances in antisemitic arson attack

A dad of four today told how he watched in horror as the ambulances burst into flames and exploded in the Golders Green attack.

‌



Max Reisner described how he heard “very, very loud explosions”. As he took this video you can hear someone screaming “fire, fire” in the background. When the third ambulance exploded he was just 15 yards away and the force of the blast threw him backwards.

‌



And he revealed that as the fire took hold elderly people in a nearby care home were taken to safety in wheelchairs. Max, 34, told the Mirror : “I saw flames and smoke billowing. Then there was a shattering third explosion as the third ambulance burst into flames."

‌



View 3 Images Max ran towards the scene of the fire and was then blown over by explosions (Image: @mosezichmir/X)

Air Canada attendant found strapped to seat outside jet after deadly crash READ MORE :

Counter-terrorism police are leading the investigation into the arson of four Jewish community ambulances in Golders Green in North London, overnight. The attack has not been declared as a terrorist incident at this stage, while officers examine the authenticity of a claim of responsibility made online. Police are looking for three hooded suspects and no arrests have yet been made.

Max told how the impact of the explosions passed through him, saying: “I felt it right in my guts and body. It was so shocking and I was afraid of course. I was not sure if there were going to be more explosions or whether buildings around the area were being attacked.

‌



“Much worse was that there were old people being taken out -- some in wheelchairs -- out of the next door block of flats by rescuers, and they must have also felt the blast as they were taken across the road to safety in a community centre.

View 3 Images Max was caught up in the blast from the ambulances

“My children went to school this morning largely unaware of what had just happened. What will they tell them at school? I don't know. My 10-year-old daughter sees pictures of children who are held hostage from the last couple of years in Gaza.

‌



“There is no easy way to tell a 10-year-old child. When she asks you, why would they kidnap children now we have evidence of the evil right in our street.”

Max was in his home nearby when he heard the fire take hold. He left and went on to the street, to be caught in the blasts from the arson attacks. He slammed those who committed the anti-Semitic outrage.

He said: “Attacking ambulances is the very worst as it these are only aimed at helping him humanity. So it is not vandalism it is terror against the community. These ambulances serve everyone whatever their tell religion or ethnicity by the way.

‌



View 3 Images The aftermath of the arson attack (Image: Ian Vogler / Daily Mirror)

“There is zero involvement in current conflicts going on abroad. As the people who volunteer for this ambulance service are only trying to help humanity. Whether it be Jews or non-Jews in various areas of London.

“The levels of hate are now not just surprising. They're also shocking. The government seems unable to stop anti-Semitism going on unchecked.

Article continues below

“People are thinking of going to live in Israel which is an active warzone rather than stay in England. I never thought I would see that. It is terrible to think this is taking place in 2026.”

News.title: 'Unclear' alert for people considering heat pumps as prices soar
News.content:
'Unclear' alert for people considering heat pumps as prices soar

Householders might be considering making a change as global energy prices rise

View 3 Images Homeowners need to check the small print (Image: Lazy_Bear via Getty Images)

As global tensions drive up oil prices, UK households are grappling with escalating costs. This has prompted many to contemplate switching to renewable energy alternatives like solar panels and heat pumps, but fresh research underscores that insurance coverage for these upgrades is often ambiguous.

‌



A study of UK home insurance products by Defaqto, an independent provider of Star Ratings for financial products, reveals that whilst products are evolving to accommodate greener technologies, there remains a lack of clarity for consumers regarding their coverage. Approximately 1.5 million homes in the UK are not connected to the gas grid and instead depend on heating oil, making them particularly susceptible to swings in global oil prices.

‌



Defaqto's research indicates that 78% of home buildings insurance products explicitly state that solar panels are included within the definition of buildings. However, 22% of products do not make any explicit reference, leaving homeowners in doubt.

‌



For ground source heat pumps, the clarity is even more scarce. Only 40% of products clearly affirm that cover is provided, while 59% do not specify whether these systems are included or excluded. A small percentage (around 1%) explicitly state that heat pumps are not covered.

In many instances, insurers may rely on general definitions of buildings, such as 'fixtures and fittings', to include these items. However, without clear wording, this can lead to confusion.

View 3 Images Solar panels might also be under consideration (Image: Justin Paget via Getty Images)

‌



Angela Pilley, home insurance expert at Defaqto, said: "As more homeowners look to invest in renewable energy solutions, it's essential they understand how these changes impact their insurance cover. While solar panels are widely recognised within building insurance products, the position for heat pumps is far less consistent.

"Where policy documents are unclear, homeowners should not assume cover is included. It's always worth checking directly with your insurer to avoid any unexpected issues at claim stage."

The study also underscores uncertainty surrounding home emergency and boiler breakdown cover. Although these products are designed to protect against heating failures, they do not always cater to newer technologies.

‌



Defaqto discovered that 39% of home emergency or boiler breakdown products do not clearly specify whether heat pumps are covered. Over half (52%) explicitly exclude them, whilst a mere 9% confirm cover, often on a reimbursement basis, meaning customers must pay upfront and reclaim the cost later.

For households still dependent on oil heating, coverage for loss of oil remains a crucial feature. At present, 91% of home contents insurance products include cover for loss of oil, either as standard (89%) or as an optional add-on (2%).

However, the extent of coverage varies greatly. Defaqto revealed that 10 per cent of products offered no cover at all and six per cent provided less than £1,000.

‌



View 3 Images A heat pump (Image: Alamy/PA)

Additionally, 44 per cent offered between £1,000 and £2,499, seven per cent ranged from £2,500 to £4,999, and 33 per cent provided £5,000 or more. The escalating cost of heating oil implies that lower limits may not fully compensate for losses in case of a leak or theft.

Angela advised: "Whether you're sticking with oil heating or investing in greener alternatives, it's important to review your insurance arrangements. Renewable upgrades can significantly increase the value of your property, so homeowners should ensure their buildings sum insured reflects these changes. It's also worth checking any existing home emergency cover, as not all products are keeping pace with newer heating technologies."

Article continues below

Advice for homeowners

Ensure solar panels or heat pumps are explicitly included in your buildings insurance

Reassess your buildings sum insured after installing renewable technology

Verify if your home emergency or breakdown cover includes heat pumps

If you depend on oil heating, check the limits for loss of oil cover

Consult your insurer if you are uncertain how upgrades impact your policy

The information was collected from the Defaqto Matrix on March 17, 2026.

News.title: Air Canada plane crash audio reveals moment controller realised he 'messed up'
News.content:
An air traffic controller said he "messed up" after a plane and a firefighting vehicle crashed at an airport, killing the pilot and co-pilot aboard the jet.

Audio released by LiveATC revealed that 20 minutes after the crash at New York's LaGuardia Airport late on Sunday, an air traffic controller said: "That wasn't good to watch." Another replied: "Yeah, I know I was here. I tried to reach out. We were dealing with an emergency earlier. I messed up.'"

The Air Canada jet carrying than 72 passengers and four crew members collided with the firefighting vehicle while landing at the airport in the northern part of the New York borough of Queens. The Jazz Aviation flight operating on behalf of Air Canada originated at Montreal-Pierre Elliott Trudeau International Airport.

The firefighting vehicle was crossing the tarmac after an air traffic controller gave it permission to respond to a United Airlines plane reporting an odour on board. Before the crash, an air traffic controller can be heard frantically telling the firefighting vehicle to stop.

Image: AP) AP)

About 40 passengers and crew members on the jet were taken to hospitals, some with serious injuries. Most were released by Monday morning, authorities said.

Images show that the impact crushed the jet’s nose, leaving cables and debris dangling from the cockpit.

The pilot and copilot who died were both based out of Canada, said Kathryn Garcia, executive director of the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey.

Two Port Authority employees in the firefighting vehicle suffered injuries that were not believed to be life-threatening, Garcia said.

"Heartbreaking news out of LaGuardia this morning," New York Governor Kathy Hochul wrote on X. "Two pilots were killed and dozens injured in this tragedy. Our thoughts are with the victims, their families, and everyone affected. The airport will remain closed until 2:00 PM. We’ll continue to share updates as they become available."

An investigation is being led by the National Transportation Safety Board.

News.title: Archaeologists prove Africa's legendary 'King Arthur' was real
News.content:
Archaeologists prove Africa's legendary 'King Arthur' was real

Earliest hard archaeological evidence of King Qashqash's reign has been found

View Image Archaeologists have unearthed a royal decree

Archaeologists have unearthed a royal decree signed by a once‑mythical African king - pulled straight from a centuries‑old rubbish heap on the banks of the Nile.

‌



The sensational find, made by a Polish team from the University of Warsaw at Old Dongola in northern Sudan, bears the name of King Qashqash - a ruler long dismissed as legend, much like Britain's King Arthur.

‌



Experts have dated the document to the late 16th or early 17th century, making it the earliest hard archaeological evidence of Qashqash's actual reign.

‌



Odds of NASA astronauts returning from Moon, AI turning us all into a brainless army, the North Sea asteroid, and why you should be grateful to have an appendix - all this and more in our latest weird science newsletter

Rather than boasting about battles, the Arabic decree, penned by a royal scribe named Hamad, orders a man called Khidr to swap textiles for livestock and hand everything to its rightful owner - capped off with a brisk command: “do not hesitate!”

Researchers say that instruction opens a window onto the real royal power and paperwork of a poorly documented era, reports The Jerusalem Post.

‌



The study calls it a “rare glimpse into Sudanic kingship during one of the least‑documented periods in Sudanese history” and, riffing on a famous turn of phrase, aims to show “the King of Nubia at work, not at war, but in everyday management".

The page turned up inside the House of the Mekk ('House of the King'), a storied complex near the Nile’s eastern bank, buried within layers of medieval refuse.

Old Dongola was once the capital of the Christian kingdom of Makuria before becoming a bustling trade hub, bridging the Ottoman Empire to the north and the Funj Sultanate to the south.

Article continues below

Alongside the page, archaeologists found several other document fragments, bits of silk, linen, and blue-dyed cloth, as well as a gold ring, a dagger handle made of ivory or rhino horn, and what appear to be musket balls and a gunpowder flask.

At the time, firearms were primarily symbols of prestige rather than weapons of war, the study explained, marking their owners as members of a powerful circle.

The discovery, published in the journal Azania: Archaeological Research in Africa, transforms Qashqash from a shadowy figure known mainly from later biographies of holy men into a documented monarch signing off royal business.

News.title: Boy, 3, dies after trainee doctor pierces artery during routine biopsy
News.content:
Boy, 3, dies after trainee doctor pierces artery during routine biopsy

Toddler Aarav Chopra died following a routine liver biopsy at Birmingham Children's hospital when he was supposed to be embarking on a 'new beginning' following a transplant

View 6 Images Aarav Chopra died from massive bleeding following what should have been a routine biopsy (Image: Irwin Mitchell / SWNS)

Parents of a three-year-old have been handed an NHS payout after their son died following what should have been routine surgery when his artery was pierced by a doctor.

‌



Aarav Chopra, three, died on November 22, 2023, from a "prolonged" cardiac arrest when a trainee medic punctured his artery, causing a buildup of blood in his chest cavity. The youngster had been suffering from serious liver disease and had undergone a liver transplant months earlier his parents had hoped would be a "new beginning".

‌



But his body rejected the new organ, and he was sent for testing at Birmingham Children's hospital, where doctors ordered a liver biopsy, a typically safe, standard procedure that went horrifically awry.

‌



View 6 Images Doctors ordered a biopsy after Aarav's body started rejecting a liver transplant (Image: Irwin Mitchell / SWNS)

A trainee doctor nicked a major artery with a needle during the surgery, and Aarav, from Wolverhampton, died of serious brain damage as doctors spent nearly 30 minutes trying to resuscitate him. An inquest into the youngster's death heard the bleed went "undiagnosed and untreated" as the biopsy was carried out.

Birmingham's Senior Coroner, Louise Hunt, also blasted "poor planning" by staff before the procedure, saying in her report that there was "poor" communication about the bleed Aarav suffered, "all of which hampered treatment after his collapse".

‌



The coroner noted there was "confusion around the experience of the trainee doctor performing the biopsy". The trainee was thought to be a year six, but it was later found out he was only in year four of his training, the inquest heard. Aarav's parents weren't told a trainee would be performing the biopsy on their son.

View 6 Images The biopsy was carried out at Birmingham Children's Hospital (Image: Anita Maric / SWNS)

Following the inquest, his parents Kishore and Amrita instructed solicitors Irwin Mitchell to investigate Aarav's care. Birmingham Women's and Children's NHS Foundation Trust have now admitted liability and paid an undisclosed pay-out to the family.

‌



It stated there "was a failure to adequately manage" Aarav's condition once the build-up of blood was recognised. With "appropriate care", Aarav's cardiac arrest and death "would have been avoided" on the balance on probabilities, the Trust said. In a letter to the couple, the Trust apologised, saying there were "failings in our care that contributed to Aarav's death."

Kishore, 38, said: "Aarav was the most adorable little boy and despite everything he went through with his transplant was always smiling and happy."

View 6 Images Aarav has been remembered as 'the most adorable little boy' (Image: Irwin Mitchell / SWNS)

‌



"He brought sunshine to our lives, and the world is a much darker place without him. We hoped his transplant would be a new start in life for Aarav. It remains difficult to accept that he went into hospital for what we thought would be a relatively routine biopsy and never came home."

The couple say their grief was made worse by the Trust's treatment of them as they tried to find out what had gone wrong. Kishore added: "After our son's death, we struggled to obtain even the most basic answers.

"The whole process was complex and overwhelming and when Birmingham Children's Hospital eventually responded, we felt there was still no accountability from them. It was only when we pushed for an independent external review and endured the distress of an inquest that the circumstances finally began to become clearer.

‌



View 6 Images Aarav's parents have said there was 'no accountability' from the Birmingham hospital (Image: Irwin Mitchell / SWNS)

"I cannot explain in words how it feels to be put in this situation as a bereaved parent." Amrita, 36, said she wanted other hospital trusts to "reflect on how they support and listen to grieving families."

She added: "I don't think we'll ever get over losing Aarav especially in the way we did. The last few years and how Aarav should be at home with us and causing mischief with his little brother has almost broken us. No parent should have to suffer the loss of a child but to then feel like you're not being listened to when raising concerns just adds to the hurt and pain."

‌



Aarav had a condition called biliary atresia - which meant the flow of bile out of his liver was blocked. He underwent a liver transplant on August 15, 2023 but he suffered complications and was prescribed antiplatelet medication to prevent blood clots.

View 6 Images Little Aarav died after he was admitted to hospital on November 20, 2023, (Image: Irwin Mitchell / SWNS)

After Aarav's body started rejecting the liver, he was admitted to hospital on November 20, 2023, for a biopsy the following day. His antiplatelet medication should have been stopped a week before the biopsy, but this wasn't done, the inquest heard. At the start of the procedure a trainee doctor inserted a needle through Aarav's rib muscles, the coroner's report said.

Article continues below

Medics didn't realise at the time that the needle "did not follow the correct pathway" and pierced Aarav's artery. Aarav went into cardiac arrest around an hour later but despite having surgery to repair his torn artery, he died the next day.

In a letter sent to Aarav's parents, Matthew Boazman and Professor Sir Bruce Keogh, the Trust's chief executive officer and chair, said: "Whilst no words will ever be adequate given your profound loss, we want to express our deep sorrow for the failings in our care that contributed to Aarav's death." The letter added that the Trust "fully accept" the coroner's conclusion and had "admitted liability in full" in the civil case.

News.title: Princess Eugenie 'hiding away' on birthday as celebrations 'fall flat'
News.content:
EXCLUSIVE: Princess Eugenie 'hiding away' on birthday as celebrations 'fall flat'

As Eugenie celebrates her 36th birthday today, the princess has stayed out of the public eye as the scandal surrounding her parents rages on

View 5 Images The youngest daughter of Andrew and Fergie has kept a low profile on her birthday (Image: Getty Images)

Princess Eugenie is celebrating her 36th birthday today, and unlike in years past, the princess has kept a low profile and remained out of the limelight. Since her father Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor’s arrest in February, Eugenie has only been seen out in public once.

‌



Just five days after Andrew was arrested on his 66th birthday on suspicion of misconduct in public office, his youngest daughter was seen dressed down buying coffee with her husband Jack Brooksbank in London's affluent Notting Hill. Before that, Eugenie was enjoying a half-term trip in the Swiss resort of Gstaad, alongside her husband Jack Brooksbank, their children August, five, and Ernest, who turns three in May.

‌



Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor installs huge mobile home on Sandringham grounds READ MORE :

View 5 Images Princess Eugenie is celebrating her 36th birthday today (Image: Getty Images)

‌



Since then, Eugenie has stayed out of the spotlight, as the Epstein scandal surrounding her parents continues to rage on. And according to former BBC royal expert Jennie Bond, Eugenie’s birthday will not stop the headlines surrounding the embattled York family.

Jennie told the Mirror : “Both Eugenie and Beatrice must be feeling the heat of the headlines that are now turning on them. And so I doubt whether this birthday will be one she will particularly feel like celebrating in a big way.”

The royal expert predicted that Eugenie would be celebrating her birthday in private with her older sister Princess Beatrice, who have been leaning on each other for support amid the news of their parents’ indiscretions.

‌



View 5 Images Beatrice and Eugenie have been supporting each other since their father's arrest (Image: PA)

“Both princesses seem to have decided that keeping a low profile for now is the best course of action,” Jennie said, “And I suspect that is how Eugenie spent her birthday: out of public view and certainly not openly with either of her parents.

“I’m sure the sisters are a great support to one another….. only they can be sure about what they know about their parents’ dubious friendships and finances.”

‌



Since both Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor and Sarah Ferguson became embroiled in the Epstein scandal, Beatrice and Eugenie have publicly shown that their loyalties lie with the Royal Family, after joining them at Sandringham for Christmas while their parents remained in exile in Windsor.

Additionally, King Charles made clear that the royal sisters would be wholly unaffected when he made the decision to strip Andrew of his royal titles late October, with Charles being known for having a good relationship with his nieces.

View 5 Images The royal sisters were wholly unaffected when Andrew was stripped of his titles last year (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

‌



However, as more allegations continue to emerge, it has been reported that Charles has been forced to keep Beatrice and Eugenie at arm’s length in order to protect the reputation of the Firm.

Calls have recently increased for Beatrice and Eugenie to speak openly about what they knew about their parents’ association with Epstein. Both of Andrew and Fergie’s daughters were named in the Epstein files, and while the inclusion of their names doesn’t indicate any wrongdoing, many have wondered about how much they know.

Given the growing calls for the royal sisters to take accountability, a royal source told the Daily Mail that the King has had no choice but to distance himself from his nieces.

‌



View 5 Images Eugenie and Beatrice spent Christmas in Sandringham with the Royal Family (Image: Getty Images)

The insider said: “Beatrice and Eugenie are particularly vulnerable at the moment. As they are dragged further into the Epstein web, the King is shifting his stance on the girls' future. Although they aren't implicated in any of Epstein's wrongdoings, their own association with him threatens to tarnish the reputation of the throne.”

Article continues below

“Both girls are very highly strung and emotional, like their mother,” says the royal source. “Beatrice is trying to come to terms with it matter-of-factly although can't help bursting into tears regularly - not for her father but for the implosion of her own world and, especially, the family's cold shoulder. Her royal status means more to her than Eugenie.” Meanwhile, the insider added that Eugenie “has apparently gone into denial - fingers in the ears, head in the sand, hoping it will all go away.”

News.title: 'Armageddon' warning as humans face 'extinction' from major threat in just a few years
News.content:
EXCLUSIVE: 'Armageddon' warning as humans face 'extinction' from major threat in just a few years

"It sounds like I'm preaching the Armageddon. And to be frank with you, to a certain degree, I am. Unless things can turn around. If we don't get our act together..."

View 5 Images Humans face extinction within just a few generations, it's been claimed (Image: Getty Images)

Humans face "absolute extinction" if fossil fuel use isn't significantly cut down, a meterology expert has warned.

‌



Our children and future grandchilden are staring down a "really terrible future" if humans fail to finally turn the tide on climate change, warned British Weather Services' Jim Dale. The entire world would slowly fall apart, little-by-little, ranging from a smallest of animals to the tallest plants, until there's nothing left, he said.

‌



His warning came after the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) revealed the world's climate is more out of balance than ever before. Rising greenhouse gas concentrations in the atmosphere have driven the changing climate, with 2025 in the top three hottest years on record, according to the UN scientists.

‌



The global average temperature last year was about 1.43C above the 1850-1990 pre-industrial average. This means the world is getting ever closer to long-term average temperatures breaching the key warming threshold of 1.5C - beyond which, increasingly severe climate impacts would be triggered.

View 5 Images Bushfires have burnt an estimated 2.7million hectares in Western Australia's Kimberley region (Image: Getty Images)

UK snow warning as Britain facing deep polar 0C freeze in DAYS as maps turn BLUE READ MORE :

"The big story is what the WMO has come out with, which is the world's energy imbalance, and temperatures at the highest for 125,000 years; particularly ocean temperatures," Mr Dale told the Mirror. "That points to extinction. And I don't say that lightly. I say it in absolute terms. I'm saying, if things continue unabated as they are, then we are facing, as a human race, potential extinction."

‌



Last year was characterised by horrifying heatwaves, widespread wildfires, hurricanes, major flooding, and extremely heavy rainfall, said the WMO. The UK was hit by a record number of summer wildfires in 2025, and major holiday hotspots - including Greece and Turkey - were both forced to shut airports over out-of-control blazes.

Meanwhile, the 2025 hurricane season generated three Category 5 storms for only the second-ever time. The season was largely remembered for Hurricane Melissa's devastation across both Jamaica and Cuba, which killed almost 100 people and cost £6.5billion in damages.

Widespread flooding in India and Pakistan killed nearly 2,000 people, triggered by pre-monsoon rain in June 2025. Just one month later, 80,000 people were displaced from China's capital city, Beijing, after heavy rain sparked city-wide floods.

‌



View 5 Images Huge craters in ice have appeared on sea that's usually covered by pack ice (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

We're forecast to see another 'El Nino' peak this year - a dramatic warming of the Pacific Ocean's surface waters - which could spark another burst of global warming. If it happens, 2027 could be the warmest year on record yet.

Mr Dale said: "What's going to happen with El Nino? That will kick on during the course of this summer into the winter. Once that kicks in... You've already seen what's going on in the United States with massive temperatures; 5C above record temperatures in places like Arizona. It's in March... this is March. It isn't July. It's just completely out of kilter.

‌



"If we don't get hold of it, then we're going to be in serious trouble. And although we're protected to a degree in the temperate world we live in, it's just a one-way street. Things will get worse and worse. That's the big story."

The global climate was now in a "state of emergency", said the UN secretary-general Antonio Guterres. Planet Earth was being pushed way beyond its limits, and "every key climate indicator was flashing red".

View 5 Images Thailand's November 2025 floods killed 178 people (Image: Getty Images)

‌



In the report, the WMO confirmed 2015 to 2025 as the hottest 11 years on record. Concentrations of heat-trapping greenhouse gases - carbon dioxide, methane and nitrous oxide - have risen to their highest level in at least 800,000 years.

Greenhouse gas emissions and concentrations have largely been sparked by fossil fuel use. But we've become so relient on fossil fuels that it would take a huge societal change to make a difference, said Mr Dale.

"Your children and your grandchildren are going to face a real terrible future if we don't get on top of fossil fuel use and thereby temperatures," he said. "But that's going to take a huge, colossal move - a bit like dragging the Titanic up from the seabed - buy we've got to drag it up and turn it around and that might do something.

‌



View 5 Images Heavy rain and persistent storms could become more common in a warmer climate (Image: Getty Images)

"We're at the beginning of it, and in the end it'll be major catastrophes - more than we've seen. That's the thing that will tip the balance and people's realisation, a bit like a war coming to them. You're not really worried about the war in, you know, Iran or Beirut until a bomb lands on London, for example."

Melting ice caps, major storms, and sea level rise were all predicted to increase with a warmer climate. All of those make life on Earth more hazardous and life-threatening, he added.

Article continues below

"It sounds like I'm preaching the Armageddon. And to be frank with you, to a certain degree, I am. Unless things can turn around. If we don't get our act together..."

News.title: HS2 trains could run slower than planned to save money
News.content: HS2's chief executive Mark Wild was expected to say this month the line would not be completed until after the current 2033 deadline and it would cost over £100bn in today's prices, but that announcement has now been delayed until after the May elections.

News.title: Is ex-Google boss Matt Brittin the right person to steer the BBC?

For me, what's become clear is that the BBC is too slow to act once mistakes are made. Look at how long it took to get the Bafta ceremony off the iPlayer, after an offensive term had been broadcast. Or how long it took to get the documentary about children in Gaza down, once it became apparent the child narrator was the son of a Hamas official.
